title: Workbook — The Desire for God
html:
Student Workbook

For use after class or in groups. Print this page or work in your notebook.

1. Recalling the Essentials

  1. What is the phrase from St. Augustine that opens the lesson? What does it mean in your own words?
  2. Explain in your own words Fulton Sheen's image of the «God-shaped hole». Why is it such a powerful image?
  3. What does the Catechism mean when it states that man is «capable» of God (CCC 27)?
  4. What are the two opposite errors about faith that the lesson points out? Explain each one briefly.
  5. The Catechism mentions seven obstacles that can prevent man from recognizing God (CCC 29). Name at least five of them.
  6. What role do witnesses and community play in the journey of faith, according to CCC 30? Why isn't it enough to search for God «on my own»?
  7. What do numbers 31 through 35 of the Catechism say about the «ways of coming to know God»? Name at least three of those ways.
  8. According to CCC 36-38, why does the Church teach that knowledge of God is not merely rational but also involves the heart and the will?

2. For Reflection and Writing

Answer each question sincerely. There are no «correct» answers to these personal questions.

1. What things do you seek to feel happy? (friends, success, rest, screens...)

Make a list of 5 things you turn to when you feel empty or lonely.

___________ ___________ ___________ ___________ ___________

2. Has any of those things given you happiness that lasts forever?

If the answer is no, why do you think we keep looking in the same places? What does this tell us about the nature of human desire?

3. Of the seven obstacles to recognizing God (CCC 29), which seems most common in your environment?

(Rebellion against evil, ignorance, indifference, riches/cares, bad example, hostile currents, sin)

Describe a concrete situation where you have seen that obstacle in action.

4. Have you ever felt that «something more» that you can't quite identify?

Describe the situation: When? Where? What were you doing? What did you feel?

5. What person in your life has helped you seek truth or ask important questions?

The Catechism says that «man needs the witness of others who teach him to seek God» (CCC 30). Who has been that witness for you? What did they do or say that left a mark on you?

6. Think about the happiest moments of your life. What did they have in common?

Do you think that in those moments you were closer to God, even if you weren't consciously thinking about it? Why?

7. Have you ever tried to «fill the hole» with something you knew wasn't right?

What happened? How did you feel afterward? What did you learn from that experience?

3. Practical Activities

A. The Map of the Heart

Draw a large heart on a sheet of paper. Divide the heart into sections like puzzle pieces. In each section, write something that occupies space in your heart: your family, your friends, your dreams, your fears, your hobbies, school or work, social media... Leave one section empty, unfilled. That section is the «God-shaped hole». Reflect and write: What are you putting there that isn't God? What would happen if you let God occupy that space? Why are you afraid to leave it empty?

B. Psalm 42 in First Person

Read Psalm 42 slowly, twice. Then write it in your own words, as if you were the one saying to God:

«As the deer longs for water, so my soul longs for you, my God...»

Continue your personal version of the psalm (6-8 lines). It's not about rhyming — it's about telling God, in your own words, what your soul feels:

 

 

 

 

 

 

C. Dialogue with Fulton Sheen

Imagine you can talk with Venerable Fulton Sheen. He tells you that you have a God-shaped hole in your soul. Write a brief dialogue (10-12 lines) between you and him. What do you ask him? What does he answer? What objections do you raise? How does the conversation end?

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

D. The Thirst Journal

Keep a small journal this week. Each night, write down:

  • What did I seek most today? (approval, fun, rest, achievement...?)
  • Did it leave me satisfied, or hungrier?
  • Was there any moment when I felt peace, even briefly? Describe it.

At the end of the week, read your whole journal and write a reflection: What pattern do you see? What direction does your deepest desire point toward?

 

 

 

 

4. Verse to Memorize

«You have made us for yourself, O Lord, and our heart is restless until it rests in you.»

— St. Augustine, Confessions I,1 (quoted in CCC 30)

Going deeper: Also read Psalm 63 («O God, you are my God, I seek you, my soul thirsts for you») and memorize verse 2. Write the verse here when you have it ready:

 

 

5. Prayer for This Week

Lord, I thank you because you made me for yourself.
I recognize that sometimes I fill my heart with things that don't satisfy me.
Help me recognize my deepest desire,
and not be afraid to seek your face.
You who are living water, quench my thirst.
Give me the courage to leave that space in my soul free
—the space that only you can fill.
Amen.

Silent prayer practice: This week, find 5 minutes a day to sit in silence. You don't have to say anything. Just sit and tell God: «Here I am.» Listen. At the end of the week, write: What happened in those minutes of silence? Was it easy or hard? Did you feel anything?

 

 

 

6. Commitment for the Week

Choose at least one concrete commitment to live out this week. Check the one you choose:

This week I will identify every time I feel that «hole» or inner emptiness and, instead of immediately filling it with my phone, food, or any distraction, I will pause for 30 seconds and say: «Jesus, this space is for you.»

I will ask someone close to me (a friend, a family member, a classmate): «Do you believe in God? Why or why not?» and I will truly listen to their answer, without judging or interrupting.

I will do the «Thirst Journal» (Activity D) for at least 5 days. Writing even one line each night.

I will pray Psalm 63 aloud twice this week, and the second time I will read it as if every word were my own.

I will visit a church or chapel for even 10 minutes, outside of Mass. Just to be there, in silence, unhurried.

My personal commitment (write it in your own words if you want something different):

 

 

At the end of the week, review: Did I keep my commitment? What changed in me by doing it? If I didn't keep it, what got in the way?

 

 

Print Workbook
